data.title: The MSC Income Fund Is Not On Par With Main Street Capital
data.description:
This year, business development companies (BIZD) have been a centerpiece of my coverage. The turmoil in direct lending has caused widespread fears across the private credit sphere and it's no secret that some non-traded business development companies have run into trouble as investors head for the hills. I recently published an article outlining the contagion effects in private credit that are beginning to weigh on publicly traded business development companies.

Just as it went with REITs when rates increased, private capital quickly faces redemption gates which put market pressure on more liquid counterparts. This factor has pushed the sector median valuation for business development companies much lower over the past year.

In today's discussion, we will compare the MSC Income Fund (MSIF) against its own BDC manager, Main Street Capital (MAIN), and explain why MSIF may be the inferior pick. MSIF went public just a short while ago and appears to have hit the public markets at a tumultuous time. Today, I will initiate coverage of MSIF, provide my take on the fund, and explain why MAIN might be a superior choice for those looking for a BDC investment.

A Short History Lesson

Before we dive into MSIF, we need to understand the relationship between MAIN and MSIF. Over the years, I have covered MAIN as a centerpiece of the business development company sector. Aside from operating a top notch business, MAIN has outperformed the sector and market as a whole over a long time period.

MAIN is an internally managed business development company that went public in 2007. Over the years, the company has differentiated itself through an equity heavy portfolio strategy that has paid dividends over the years, quite literally. The fund has been one of the more unique business development companies in that net asset value has grown significantly over time allowing the company to grow its dividend and provide special dividends to shareholders. Within the business development company space, MAIN has historically focused on smaller, but mature businesses. In the words of the company, they focus on businesses that are older than they are.

This successful track record has helped the company amass nearly $9 billion under management. Around 80% of this capital sits under the umbrella of the fund at MAIN. The remaining portion was mostly held in a non-traded business development company to which MAIN was the fund advisor. This smaller piece allowed MAIN to act as an advisor providing asset management services that include sourcing, due diligence, and investment management. Meanwhile, the former partner to the fund handled fundraising, balance sheet management, and accounting and administrative services.

At the time, the fund was called the Main Street Income Fund. In October 2020, MAIN became the sole advisor to the fund and the name changed to the MSC Income Fund. At this point, MAIN had itself its own non-traded business development company of which it was the external asset manager. MAIN is a business development company that is internally managed. This means that the company does not charge a management and incentive fee to assets under management but rather operates more like a traditional business. Within that internally managed business sat this externally managed fund of which MAIN was the fund advisor and collected management fees, a boost to their earnings.

MAIN's investor presentation quotes that this reduces their effective expenses on total assets to 1.3%. Compare this point to MAIN's estimate of other BDC's which have an effective expense ratio of around 2.5%.

Last year, MAIN took this non-traded BDC public under the ticker symbol MSIF.

So, to summarize, MSIF was formerly a non-traded business development company under the umbrella of MAIN. Following the IPO at the beginning of last year, it became a separate, externally managed, publicly traded business development company that continues to be overseen by the team at MAIN. This opens up immediate liquidity for the fund's investors. Rather than being held under the internal expense structure of MAIN, MSIF pays asset management fees to MAIN.

MAIN discusses the benefit to their business on the Q4 Earnings Call.

Funds we advised through our external investment manager continued to experience favorable performance in the fourth quarter, resulting in significant incentive fee income for our asset management business, and together with our recurring base management fees, a significant contribution to our net investment income.

MSIF's current base management fee is 150 basis points according to the most recent investor presentation. As MSIF continues to grow, this will become a growing piece of MAIN's business.

This should hopefully provide a quick overview of this short but exciting history of this externally managed business development company. With that, let's go ahead and dive into the fund and see how it stacks up against other BDC's.

Fund Overview

Since MSIF is an independent fund from MAIN, we should assess it on its own. The fund manages a total investment portfolio of about $1.3 billion. Like the original MAIN, the fund has a split between debt investments and equity investments. However, unlike its older sibling, this fund has a much smaller portion of the portfolio allocated towards equity at around 20%.

I would venture to guess as the fund begins to age and move towards the maturity of its publicly traded stage, we will see a much, much smaller equity portion within the portfolio. This assertion is supported by the fund's website which explains that MSIF differs from MAIN through what the manager calls a "private loan only investment strategy"

MSIF differs from Main Street Capital through its private loan only investment strategy and focus on providing current returns through current dividends.

This is an important distinction to draw as two key drivers of man's ability to grow net asset value over time have been appreciating equity investments and the ability to leverage a premium valuation to issue shares well above net asset value. Without a substantial equity sleeve to support operations, MSIF looks more like other business development companies that are publicly traded.

As is typical with the MAIN secret recipe, MSIF invests and established, profitable companies. Below is a screenshot from the most recent investor presentation that talks about the private loan, lower middle market, and aggregate portfolio investments.

As one would expect with any business development company, investments are spread across a wide variety of clients. The total portfolio has nearly 150 companies with an average investment size of approximately $9 million. Private loans make up around 61% of assets and lower middle market comprises around 36%. The remaining small portion is allocated to the middle market and other portfolio investments.

Right now, the fund is well diversified across industries with technology comprising a relatively small component of the overall portfolio. Having a larger portion of the portfolio exposed to fixed rate debt means the fund is more sensitive to movements in benchmark interest rates as well. MSIF provides a SOFR sensitivity that shows net investment income per share moves alongside SOFR.

Most of these loans will come from MAIN's deals as MSIF continues to shift towards their debt only strategy. Specifically, the fund's private loan strategy which is the largest piece of the portfolio is directly tied to MAIN.

Private Loan portfolio investments are primarily debt investments in privately held companies which have primarily been originated directly by Main Street or, to a lesser extent, through Main Street's strategic relationships with other credit funds on a collaborative basis (often referred to in the debt markets as "club deals" because of the small lender group size)

Let's explore this relationship more deeply.

Putting The Pieces Together

This relationship becomes collaborative with MAIN, who can retain the equity position while having the opportunity to sell loans directly into MSIF instead of finding an external buyer.

Think of it this way, MAIN is a private equity engine which has been able to assemble a large portfolio of companies that they control. A cornerstone of MAIN's business has been collecting dividends from these companies which helps support the normal and special distribution paid by MAIN shares. Over time, debt investments have remained a large portion of man's investment strategy. This separate externally managed pool was formerly comprised of external capital. It provided an opportunity for MAIN to allocate their investments and other investments they come across into an external capital pool where it might not necessarily fit on their own balance sheet.

Overtime, this flexible strategy has been adapted by other companies including Realty income which has recently introduced a non-traded REIT to their own platform under a similar concept.

The issue that was facing MAIN with this externally managed, non-traded business development company may have been liquidity. By having external investors, these investors may want to pull their capital, something which we have seen happening on an increasing basis. This year, redemption gates have been coming up quickly for non-traded business development companies as investors quickly try to pull out their capital. By converting to a permanent public structure MSIF is able to guarantee a permanent capital structure which is advantageous for the long term hold period expected for lower middle market investments. This is a huge advantage for a fund advisor at a time like this when liquidity becomes an issue.

However, this means the new company is exposed to the public markets and their corresponding turmoil. Unfortunately for investors who piled in quickly, this has quickly translated to a discounted valuation as the fund's price continues to move below net asset value.

MSIF shares continue to trade at a discount to net asset value alongside other business development companies as the sector faces trouble.

Many see this as a huge opportunity as MAIN itself continues to trade far above its own net asset value.

Let me explain why this is so important for MAIN. Over the past few years, MAIN has been rapidly issuing shares at a premium to net asset value.

This capital market activity in and of itself raises net asset value as I have explained in my coverage of MAIN.

Historically, MAIN has earned this valuation because of its ability to grow net asset value with the equity sleeve. Then it compounded when the equity sleeve worked alongside share issuances as a double whammy.

Now, it appears that MAIN is only able to grow NAV through this capital market activity as the going gets tough. As nonaccruals continue to grow and valuations come under pressure, net asset value growth is carrying the entire load for NAV growth. See below from MAIN's recent preliminary NAV estimates.

The estimated NAV per share increase is primarily due to the accretive impact of equity issuances, partially offset by the net fair value decrease of the existing investment portfolio, the total dividends per share paid in the first quarter in excess of NII per share and the net tax provision.

Well, trading at a material discount to net asset value means that MSIF simply cannot pull this same lever to deliver returns to shareholders. What quickly begins to look even worse is the fund's pivot deeper and deeper into debt investments while nonaccrual begins to rise.

Investments on nonaccrual have almost reached 4% of cost, nearly matching that of MAIN itself. Right now, this leaves MSIF in a disadvantageous position relative to MAIN in terms of their abilities to grow net asset value.

Recently, my assessment of MAIN has been somewhat negative, earning a Sell rating due to valuation concerns.

More recently, shifts in pricing have roughly realigned share price with historical price to book value. With the calculus around these two shifting, the picture begins to look somewhat different. For example, today, MAIN trades at around 1.5x forward book value, below the 1.67x five year average for MAIN shares.

Investor Takeaways

When people are looking at MSIF and MAIN, I am concerned that people believe they are similar. While there are some folks who call them MAIN and mini-MAIN, I believe a better comparison is Gladstone Capital (GLAD) relative to Gladstone Investment (GAIN) which have similarly differentiated strategies. Looking at these two specifically, we can see just how far performance can diverge over time.

When people are comparing MSIF to MAIN, it's important to incorporate the difference in management style, portfolio strategy, and balance sheet. These critical differences are likely to become the determining factor in long term performance when combined with the fund's ability to leverage capital markets activity and manage price relative to net asset value. All of these factors amount to considerable risks for a fund which is yet to prove itself as a publicly traded vehicle. With expenses that are considerably higher than MAIN and a less attractive strategy overall, MSIF earns a Sell rating, while MAIN earns a Hold based on current pricing.

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners: The Valuation Remains Challenging
data.description:
With the Iran war still threatening to spark a bout of stagflation, trading conditions could get more difficult for Europe's consumer-facing businesses. That being the case, honing in on those firms with the strongest brands may not be the worst idea in the world.

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners PLC (CCEP) is one possible candidate. Few brands are as iconic as its namesake, and while CCEP doesn't actually own it, its status as the largest Coke bottler in the world puts it on the front line in terms of consumer demand.

I last covered CCEP in 2024. Downgrading it to "Hold", my major issue was with its valuation. As expected, CCEP's volumes had been holding up well following meaningful price hikes, but given the low-growth nature of its markets and beverage portfolio, I didn't want to pay more than 15x earnings for the shares.

Returning around 35%, they have done well since then. Some of that is due to currency (the dollar has weakened), but re-rating has also played a role, with CCEP now trading for around 20x trailing earnings, up from 18.5x last time. Frankly, this remains a sticking point for me. With better value nearby in the Coca-Cola system, I continue to rate CCEP "Hold."

Brand Strength

To quickly recap, CCEP is one of the largest bottlers in the Coca-Cola system. Its business model is pretty simple. The company buys ingredients and finished goods under license agreements from the better-known Coca-Cola Company (KO), turns them into end products, and then ships them to commercial customers in various channels (supermarkets, wholesalers, hotels, and so on).

Last year, CCEP booked €21 billion in revenue. Around 75% came from Europe, where CCEP operates in mature northern and western markets. The rest was generated in Asia and Oceania, where CCEP has exposure to faster-growing emerging markets like Indonesia and the Philippines.

Unfortunately, the economic outlook in Europe is highly uncertain. Just like in 2022, when the Russian invasion of Ukraine triggered a surge in inflation, the conflict in Iran is threatening the same. CCEP's non-European markets aren't immune either. In fact, they may fare even worse. The Philippines, for example, introduced a four-day week last month to conserve energy.

It helps that the Coca-Cola brand is so strong. Put simply, private label penetration is quite low in the soda category, making Coke relatively more immune to down-trading compared to other food & beverage products.

Evidence to support this can be found in CCEP and KO's recent historical results. Between 2023 and 2025, for example, CCEP managed to deliver flat comparable unit case growth despite growing its revenue per case by an average of 4%. KO's results are even starker. Over the same period, it delivered 1%-2% average unit case growth while delivering 8% average growth in price/mix.

Two Structural Reasons For Caution

Now, you will note above that the drivers of CCEP's top line have been weaker than KO's. There are two structural reasons contributing to this. Firstly, CCEP's operating footprint largely covers very mature beverage markets. As you saw in the last section, Great Britain, France, Germany, Spain, and Portugal account for well over half its revenue. These markets have unfavorable demographics and mature rates of soda consumption.

By contrast, Indonesia and the Philippines enjoy better prospects. Demographics are healthier, as is the outlook for consumption, given low per capita rates compared to Western standards (shown below). Unfortunately, these two markets only account for around 12% of CCEP's revenue, making their impact relatively limited.

The second reason is related to CCEP's beverage mix. Classic Coca-Cola is around 35-40% of its volume, rising to 60% including low/zero-sugar formulations like Coke Zero and Diet Coke. Flavors and Mixers, which includes non-Coke soda brands like Sprite and Fanta, is another 20% of its volume. That makes for a total skew to the sparkling sub-category of approximately 80%. Now, while this helps CCEP fend off competition from private label products, it does come with a flip side, which is that the sparkling category has one of the weakest growth outlooks in the coming years.

Combined, these points do not make for an exciting revenue outlook for CCEP. Indeed, sales only grew by around 3% last year after adjusting for currency and the impact of M&A. Analysts expect a similar rate of growth through 2028, including in its Q1 2026 update at the end of the month.

It's true that profit growth has been higher than this. CCEP posted €2.81 billion in operating income last year, up 7% after adjusting for currency and M&A. Analysts expect a similar rate of growth through 2028. Operating leverage is part of this, while CCEP has been able to extract cost efficiencies throughout its business (more automation in its warehouses, for example). Eventually, though, earnings growth will revert closer to revenue growth.

War Impacts Won't Show Up Just Yet

Having mentioned the upcoming Q1 report, it's worth spending a brief moment to discuss the implications of events in the Middle East. Before that, I should point out that this will only be a trading update—meaning we won't get a comprehensive set of financial data (only figures for revenue).

With that, analysts expect 9% currency-neutral revenue growth, driven by 7.5% volume growth. Now, these figures seem high in light of what I set out in the last section, but this growth is really just a phasing effect related to how CCEP's financial calendar works. It was mentioned on the previous earnings call, but to cut a long story short, there will be six extra selling days in Q1 2026.

I am not expecting any major headwinds from Iran to show up in this update. Not only did the conflict begin towards the end of the quarter, but recent commentary from British supermarket chain Tesco (TSCDY) suggests that consumers have yet to alter their behavior. Note that Britain is CCEP's biggest market, and as Tesco is the largest supermarket chain there, its insights are relevant to CCEP.

Look, let me keep it in simple terms. So far, we haven't seen any real discernible change in consumer spending behaviors, right? You see that in our Kantar data. You see it in both the volume and the value share. I feel good about how we started the year, but it's early days, and I would say to you that we aim to grow profits every single year.

Imran Nawaz, Tesco CFO & Director.

On the flip side, GDP forecasts from organizations like the IMF have been lowered, and that could obviously weigh on consumer demand later in the year. Note also that CCEP has already hedged around 80% of its 2026 commodity costs, and these account for around 25% of its direct costs. This will limit the impact of any price shocks on its cost base, at least for now.

Given the above, it would not surprise me if CCEP widened its guidance to account for the extra uncertainty, but I am not expecting major revisions at this point.

A Puzzling Premium

The above is key to assessing CCEP's valuation. At $96.78 (around €82 in its operating currency), the shares trade for roughly 20x last year's adjusted EPS (€4.11). Not only does this look lofty in its own right, but this multiple is also puzzling compared to one of its closest bottling peers, Coca-Cola HBC (CCHGY)(CCHBF), which I covered last week. This is what I was referring to when I said that there was better value nearby in the Coca-Cola system.

Like CCEP, Coca-Cola HBC also has a large European footprint, albeit it operates in southern, central, and eastern markets. However, around 27% of Coca-Cola HBC's volumes come from Nigeria and Egypt, where both demographics and consumption rates are tailwinds rather than headwinds. Coca-Cola HBC has also agreed to a deal to buy Coca-Cola Beverages Africa ('CCBA'), which will add another 14 African markets to its list of territories. Post-deal, over 40% of Coca-Cola HBC's volume will come from Africa.

In addition to that, I would also add that Coca-Cola HBC's portfolio is slightly less reliant on the sparkling sub-category, which accounts for around 67% of its volume. By contrast, it has slightly more exposure to faster-growing products like energy drinks (9% of the portfolio versus 5% for CCEP). Consistent with all of this, analysts expect Coca-Cola HBC to grow its revenue and operating profit at a faster clip than CCEP in the coming years.

You might expect this growth to attract a higher valuation from the market. In fact, Coca-Cola HBC's ADSs trade for $59.25 (around €50 in its operating currency), equal to around 18.5x last year's adjusted EPS (€2.72). While there are other factors that influence valuation, these don't appear to explain things either. Leverage, for example, is roughly comparable, with CCEP sitting on €9.8 billion in net debt at the end of 2025, equal to roughly 2.7x that year's EBITDA. Coca-Cola HBC's leverage was only 0.7x at the end of last year, though it is set to rise to 2.5x EBITDA post-CCBA.

Given inferior growth, comparable leverage (pro forma for the CCBA deal), and a higher P/E ratio, I don't see a compelling reason for prospective investors to favor CCEP over Coca-Cola HBC at this point. Accordingly, I leave my "Hold" rating for CCEP unchanged.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Why I See Downside Ahead
data.description:
So, since my last article, "Johnson & Johnson: Strong Run May Be Ending," which, by the way, caused an extremely negative reaction from you, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) aka J&J stock has fallen 4.2%, underperforming the S&P 500 [1.8% return] (SPY).

And one of the reasons behind this is the mixed Q1 numbers. Though it is not obvious at first glance.

Yes, its revenue grew 9.9% year-over-year to $24.06 billion, beating the consensus estimate by $447 million. And yes, its non-GAAP EPS of $2.7 was also higher than what Wall Street analysts expected, but just by 2 cents.

And so, I have a question. Is J&J doing well with sales of its immunology, oncology, and neurology franchises?

Or is there still a divergence between sales of its key blockbusters and the price of its shares, which prevents an upgrade rating of Johnson & Johnson under my “ALLKA 4D Rating” model?

J&J's immunology franchise

First of all, let me remind you that Tremfya and Stelara are two key drugs in its portfolio.

There's also Remicade [infliximab].

But biosimilars to it have already been launched.

And Alvotech/Teva Pharmaceuticals' AVT05 (ALVO, TEVA) and Bio-Thera's BAT2506 are awaiting FDA approval. By the way, AVT05/BAT2506 are biosimilars to J&J's golimumab.

So, as I said earlier, Tremfya [guselkumab] is the successor to the fast-fading blockbuster Stelara.

Since 2025, 8 "cheap copies" of Stelara have already been launched, including Amgen's Wezlana (AMGN), Samsung Bioepis/Sandoz's Pyzchiva (SDZNY), and Biocon Biologics' Yesintek.

That is why sales of this IL-12/23 inhibitor fell by 59.6% YoY and 46.7% quarter-over-quarter to $656 million, and also missed even my "base case" scenario by $55 million.

On the other hand, Tremfya, a first anti-IL-23 mAb, showed modest performance, even though the FDA on September 29 last year approved its subcutaneous version for the treatment of children over 6 years of age with two forms of immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, plaque psoriasis and active psoriatic arthritis.

I wrote "modest performance" for a reason.

So, Tremfya generated $1.61 billion in revenue for J&J, a 1.2% quarter-on-quarter growth.

The slowdown in its sales, in my view, reflects the preference of gastroenterologists for AbbVie's Rinvoq/Skyrizi (ABBV) and Takeda's (TAK) Entyvio.

And also, the FDA approved Eli Lilly's (LLY) Omvoh on January 15 of last year for the treatment of Crohn's disease.

The oncology franchise has once again demonstrated strong performance

I can say for sure that J&J is the undisputed leader in the multiple myeloma and lung cancer therapeutics market.

And behind this lies the "Fantastic Four": Darzalex/Darzalex Faspro [daratumumab], Lazcluze/Rybrevant, Carvykti, and Tecvayli.

But I'll only discuss daratumumab and Carvykti, as they are J&J's 2 best-selling cancer drugs, if I don’t take Imbruvica [ibrutinib] into account.

Overall, as you may know, Imbruvica is under pressure from more effective inhibitors of BTK, BeOne Medicines' Brukinsa (ONC) and AstraZeneca's Calquence (AZN). That’s why ibrutinib revenues were down 6.9% YoY.

But this is not all.

In my opinion, it's also important to pay attention to the factors that could cause stock trading at a non-GAAP P/E ratio [FWD] higher than their sector median and 5-year average, as in J&J's case [15.6% and 24%, respectively], to fall in price.

So I'll continue with Darzalex.

At first glance, everything looks great. The daratumumab franchise generated $3.96 billion in sales for Johnson & Johnson in Q1, up 22.5% year-over-year.

But this morning, I created the following scatter plot, which helped me put all the pieces of the puzzle together.

What do I see here, below?

Before I answer, I'll explain how this chart works, just in case. The text above the circles indicates the quarter I'm comparing it to.

For example, the purple dot indicates on the X-axis how sales of Darzalex/Darzalex Faspro changed in Q1 2026 compared to Q4 2025 [grew by $61 million]. And if you do the same on the Y-axis, it will show the change in sales YoY, that is, Q1 2026 relative to Q1 2025 [up $727 million]. The colors of the circles indicate the year [red is 2025 and purple is 2026].

And yes, VP Darren Snellgrove sounded quite enthusiastic about Darzalex on the call.

In oncology, starting with multiple myeloma, DARZALEX growth was 17.8%, primarily driven by strong share gains of 5.9 points across all lines of therapy, with nearly 12 points in the frontline setting as well as market growth.

Therefore, I can only assume that the slowdown in the sales of the daratumumab franchise is either a one-off incident or related to oncologists starting to choose Sanofi's Sarclisa (SNY), also the anti-CD38 therapy as Darzalex, and J&J's other anti-multiple myeloma [MM] agents, Tecvayli and Carvykti.

That's why I'll continue talking about Carvykti [ciltacabtagene autoleucel].

This CAR T-cell therapy is approved as 2L+ relapsed and lenalidomide-refractory MM.

I have already mentioned how Carvykti works in previous articles, but its important advantage compared to Darzalex or Talvey or Pfizer's Elrexfio (PFE) is that it is a one-time infusion.

And, in my view, also because of the expansion of manufacturing capacity, including at the Tech Lane facility in Ghent, Belgium, and the Raritan facility, NJ, Carvykti sales rose by 61.8% YoY and by 7.6% quarter-on-quarter to $597 million in Q1.

But again, there's a small detail I should mention, namely, J&J only earns 50% from Carvykti sales, under the collaboration agreement with Legend Biotech (LEGN).

Thus, I believe, although I would like to point out that this is only my subjective opinion, that Legend is a suitable acquisition target, especially in anticipation of the results of the two Phase 3s, CARTITUDE-5 and CARTITUDE-6.

I base my opinion on the fact that LEGN's market capitalization is only $3.8 billion, even though it has a rich portfolio of product candidates in addition to Carvykti. For example, LCAR-AIO [R/R autoimmune diseases] or LB2102 [large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma and small cell lung cancer].

And moreover, Gilead (GILD) acquired Legend's competitor, Arcellx (ACLX), for $7.8 billion, even though its anti-BCMA CAR T-cell agent, anito-cel, is still under review by the FDA.

Risks

Below, I've highlighted 6 key risks.

Takeaway

Johnson & Johnson is the "gold standard" among Big Pharma players for income-oriented investors.

But I started to see several warning signs.

As I mentioned in the article, three of the four drugs in J&J's immunology franchise, Stelara, Remicade, and Simponi/Simponi Aria, are experiencing or will soon experience pressure from their biosimilars in the US.

For the first time in 9 years, it increased its quarterly dividends by only 4 cents.

I also didn't hear any significant progress on resolving over 70,000 talc cases on the earnings call.

As a result, I continue to cover J&J with a Sell rating.

However, I must point out that I may reconsider it if the stock reaches the +1σ band formed from the VWAP [$214.2]. Or if Johnson & Johnson finally buys out either Legend or Protagonist (PTGX), which will rejuvenate its pipeline.

data.title: NextEra Energy: The Utility Story Just Changed (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My previous article

I previously wrote about NextEra Energy in June 2025. My assessment was "Hold". To be honest, I was conservative. This wasn't because I did not have confidence in the quality of the company. On the contrary. For me NextEra was (and still is) a good stock. Perhaps even better than what the market currently prices it at. That's exactly why I made that point so clear in the June piece. As I mentioned before, FPL offers regulated stability and high-quality operations. NEER has the opportunity for growth and the renewable energy narrative. However, the valuation of the stock had already priced most of that into it. With multiples near peer levels, consistently negative FCF, and a capital structure that seemed unaccommodating to rising rates. Therefore, I wanted to wait for a lower entry point, which I believed would occur at or around $65.

Looking back now, the markets disagreed with my timing. The stock rose from its opening price of $71.51 to $91.83, which equates to an increase of approximately 28.4% for the year and far exceeded the 14.5% total return of the S&P 500 over the same time frame. Yes, I am admitting here that I left an opportunity on the table as a result. But I think I will be truthful instead of pretending like it never occurred.

What changed?

With that being said, I am coming back to write about this stock and I'm not returning to the stock today because it suddenly became cheap. It really doesn't look that cheap. However, since June, I think that the story of NextEra has grown considerably. NextEra is not simply a premium utility with an outstanding regulated business in Florida and a large-scale renewables division. It is evolving to become an electric platform for the AI and data center boom. FPL will remain the stable foundation of the two businesses, with over six million customer accounts and 37 GW in service.

NEER will provide national scale, backlog, storage, transmission, gas, and nuclear capabilities. According to NextEra's March investor presentation, they are identifying twelve different avenues for expansion and targeting at least fifteen GW of new power plant capacity associated with data centers by 2035. With potential upside to thirty GW depending upon how fast those developments grow.

To me that is what makes the biggest difference versus June: the quality was there last June. The bigger, bolder growth plan is what is making a difference today in my mind. At least to some extent, this larger growth vision isn't currently embedded in the value that many investors assign to the stock.

A larger and more authentic story

In my opinion, the main reason why we are seeing the change in the market's perception of NextEra since June, is due to the shift from viewing the company merely as a premium utility run efficiently (the prior narrative), to one where NextEra is viewed as a scalable electricity platform for the booming AI and data center space.

According to NextEra’s March investor presentation, the company’s main growth opportunity is its ability to deliver between 15 GW and 30 GW of new data center-related capacity by 2035. Additionally, the company has outlined a gas-fired generation pipeline that exceeds 20 GW. Moreover, NextEra has entered into a Memorandum of Understanding ("MOU") with Xcel to expedite the development of generation resources that can meet the needs of large-load customers. In the January earnings call, NextEra disclosed that there were approximately 9 GW of large-load development opportunities in advanced discussions. With respect to future opportunities, NextEra also discussed the possibility of increasing nuclear capacity to service these anticipated additional loads. In summary, based upon my understanding of events, this is the major change in how the market perceives NextEra since June; it is no longer simply a quality story, it is a story about industrial scale being applied to a rapidly expanding industry.

I also believe that the expansion of this more ambitious narrative has occurred in tandem with financial results that have held up thus far. Following a record fourth quarter in 2025 (with adjusted EPS of $0.54) that exceeded the analysts' estimates (by one cent), the company concluded the year with adjusted EPS of $3.71, representing an increase of more than eight percent compared to the same period in the prior year.

For fiscal year 2026, the company reaffirmed its previously issued guidance for adjusted EPS ($3.92-$4.02). The company continues to target the upper-end of this range. To me, what is important regarding this matter is that such results make the narrative less speculative. Adding to that, in the past twelve months, NextEra shares have increased by 42.6%. During this same time frame, the average rating of analysts covering NextEra (as calculated by Bloomberg) remained constructive. Therefore, it appears obvious to me that the valuation multiple did not occur by accident.

Good 4Q25

NextEra Energy’s 4Q25 results were solid, with bottom-line performance modestly exceeding consensus estimates. I'm interested in the company’s ability to execute on its growth strategy, and the Q4 report showed NextEra continuing to deliver solid operating fundamentals while growing its project backlog.

On an adjusted basis, NextEra reported a $0.54 EPS vs. $0.53 a year ago. On a full-year basis, NextEra reported a $3.71 EPS ($3.43 prior year), a roughly +8.2% increase, and delivered revenue of $27.4 billion (+10.7%). While these figures may not be exciting by themselves, when taken as a whole, they represent a strong delivery on NextEra’s stated goals and reinforce our view of the company as a solid operator with a long runway for future growth.

One area of interest to me at this stage is Florida Power & Light Company (“FPL”), which I believe represents the true strength of NextEra’s overall story. FPL reported a $2.42 per-share result in 2025 vs. $2.21 in 2024, with adjusted capital growth of about +8.1% and annual cap-ex of around $8.9 billion. Put simply, FPL continues to perform its role as the stable engine of the company, investing aggressively and delivering financial returns without compromising either its operational performance or customer pricing objectives, both key elements to NextEra’s competitive advantage.

On the other hand, NextEra Energy Resources ("NEER") is where things get very interesting, but also slightly riskier. As expected, NEER reported a positive result for 2025, with adjusted EPS coming in at $1.70 vs. $1.51 in 2024. More important, however, is what happened downstream, as the company added ~13.5GW of new projects to the backlog during 2025 (~3.6GW in the final quarter). At nearly 30GW today, we continue to believe this backlog, combined with management's increasing focus on hyperscalers, large loads, gas, transmission, and nuclear, presents the greatest value opportunity within NextEra.

I find the outlook provided by management to be reasonable and conservative in nature, particularly in light of the current backdrop for utilities. Rather than providing aggressive “hero” type numbers for 2026, management provided a clearly defined outlook, confirming an adjusted EPS range of $3.92-$4.02 for 2026, and reaffirmed its goal of achieving >8% compound annual earnings growth rates throughout each of 2032 and 2035, based upon a 2025 baseline of $3.71. Simply put, the story remains unchanged, and now it's time for management to prove that the contracts supporting their vision are truly behind them.

Valuation

The first thing I want to say is that there is little use in beating around the bush regarding Nextera. Nextera is not inexpensive. As shown by the Seeking Alpha screenshot, Nextera has been assigned a valuation grade of D- and the reasons why are self-evident. The stock trades for approximately 22 times its estimated forward earnings.

On the other hand, its peers such as Duke Energy (DUK), Southern Company (SO), and Dominion Energy (D) trade for lower multiples. The stock’s EV/EBITDA ratio is also higher than most of its peers.

Specifically, Nextera trades at approximately 16.2 times next twelve-months (NTM) EBITDA whereas the three-year average for its peer group is closer to 15x. Therefore, we can see that we’re not talking about a low-cost utility.

However, I do believe it would be too easy to simply stop there. In other words, while I agree that the valuation is relatively expensive compared to where it has been over time, I also believe that the valuation does not appear as distorted today as it appeared several months ago. For example, the forward Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple has decreased since peaking at around 30x to 22.9x. At the same time, investors have started to put more confidence in the company’s expected long-term performance. In addition to a decrease in the P/E multiple, some de-risking has already taken place because the multiple has normalized, but the industrial story behind Nextera has improved since last quarter.

As stated earlier, the investors are ready to offer a higher price, a premium paid for a utility that includes:

  1. A very large and well-regulated base in Florida.
  2. Approximately 30GW of backlog related to renewable energy generation and battery storage.
  3. An increasingly important natural gas transmission system.
  4. Long-term expected earnings per share (EPS) growth rates of greater than 8% per annum through 2035, which was also reiterated during the company’s recent March presentation.

3 Risks

In my view, the three major threats to the bullish case for NextEra energy are:

Execution

Although the markets have certainly paid a price for the narratives around "data center" and "large load," as well as "gas", the ~9 gw of advanced discussion data centers in Florida will need to convert into signed agreements; likewise, the 4-8 gw of new gas generation required by 2030-32 also need to convert into signed agreements. If that does slow down significantly, then the bull story begins to lose steam.

High valuation

NextEra continues to trade at a premium to its peers, even after it has normalized from its current valuation. Therefore, there is limited margin for error: one less-than-clean quarter, one more conservative forecast than expected, or one operational delay could cause the multiple on nee to compress.

Future growth

While the target of 8+% CAGR in earnings per share (EPS) by 2035 is a credible one, it will require all of several levers to work together: large load FPL, transmission, nuclear recontracting, storage & gas. While direction is right, much of future growth will still need concrete execution to fill it.

Conclusion

I was too cautious last time. That happens. I missed part of the move, and I am not going to rewrite history and pretend otherwise.

As a result, I don't believe we are in a situation where we should be chasing this stock with both hands down from a strong move. NextEra Energy is not at dirt-cheap levels. The premium is built into the price. The markets have already given management plenty of value for the potential future success they will likely experience within the next few years. However, I believe the story has evolved, and it is larger and more credible than it was when I gave the stock a rating of "Hold." FPL is still providing stability to the entire operation. In addition to being a renewable energy growth stock, NEER is now offering shareholders an opportunity to invest in more than just a general renewable energy growth story. Backlog, Transmission & Gas, Nuclear Power & Data Center Buildouts offer an unambiguous direction for investors.

Management continues to guide toward an adjusted EPS range of $3.92- $4.02 in 2026 and 8% + sustained growth through 2035. FPL's Large Load Discussions & NEER's approximately 30GW backlog continue to provide a reason to remain bullish about the midterm for NEE.

Now, what am I doing differently? I am upgrading my recommendation to buy, but I am approaching this like a portfolio manager rather than a momentum trader. I do not currently hold shares of NEE, and I do not intend to take on a full-size position immediately following a move like this. Instead, I plan to start small.

So, I like the setup. I appreciate how management has been executing thus far, but I continue to seek discipline regarding the way I enter investments.

data.title: Strait Of Hormuz - Impact Of A Long-Term Closure
data.description:
To say the War with Iran is a fluid situation would be an understatement. The news changes by the day, and even in any given day, official estimates can vary from "war should end soon" to "a deal will take 6 months". And any deal that seems to be imminent could fall apart at any moment.

My hope is this article will still be relevant by the time it is published.

The goal of this article is to game out one of the worst case scenarios where there is impasse on reaching a deal, the US blockade persists, and the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed for a long period of time (>1 year).

Under this scenario, what are the impacts to oil supply, and what are some investments that will benefit?

Capitalism Finds a Way

Pre-war, roughly 20M barrels per day (b/d) of crude oil and refined petroleum products shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, or about 20% of global petroleum consumption - which, if fully disrupted, sounds like an apocalyptic number, until you consider what happened during other similar commodity disruptions such as the Russia/Ukraine war.

At the onset of Russia/Ukraine war, some people were predicting massive disruption in fertilizers and energy that would've caused a surge in famine and the deindustrialization of Western Europe. However, through a combination of supply diversification, demand reductions, policy interventions, and adaptive market responses, the worst predictions were largely averted.

Similarly, let's objectively evaluate how the market may adapt to the conflict with Iran.

Saudi and Nearby Pipelines

The largest single bypass is the east-west Saudi pipeline that redirects products to the Red Sea. It is now running at capacity - 5M b/d of crude goes directly to export, and another 2M b/d gets refined domestically before being exported as petroleum products, for a total of 7M b/d.

UAE's ADCOP and other smaller pipelines in the area redirects another 2M b/d to detour around Hormuz. Let's estimate the total diverted to be around 8-9M b/d.

Venezuela Ramp-up

Since the US military operations on Venezuela this year, previous oil production from that country that was either sanctioned or blockaded are being brought back online, and further investments from US oil majors are expected to ramp-up production beyond the current 1-1.2M b/d level.

Venezuela has the world's largest proven reserves - decades ago their production peaked at 4M b/d level. Ramp up will be gradual but we can reasonably estimate an additional 0.5-1M b/d being available in over a year's time from a combination of production increase and exports coming off sanction, especially if higher oil price incentivizes investments to be made quicker.

US Shale Production Surge

The US shale industry is very price-sensitive, so one should expect the elevated oil price (at the current $90-100 range) to motivate greater rig starts, bringing additional supply online.

EIA estimates a delta of 500,000 b/d production increase by next year compared to their older Feb estimate due to disruptions in the Middle East.

OPEC+/Non-OPEC Production Increase

In addition to US, EIA expected net production increase from OPEC+, Argentina, Brazil, and Guyana of 0.8M b/d prior to the war. Another 0.1M b/d is expected to be available from Canada by deferring oil sand maintenance. Given higher prices now, I estimate overall production increase to amount to roughly 1M b/d.

Selective Passage

Since the conflict started, certain vessels were allowed to pass through Hormuz either by paying Iran a toll, or by loading up from UAE ports to avoid US blockade.

I estimate about 1-1.5M b/d on average was flowing through Hormuz while traffic remains restricted.

Demand Displacement

Another major market-balancing factor often missed is demand that is displaced or replaced permanently by alternatives.

As oil price stays elevated, there has been clear and widespread evidence of surging interest in EVs (electric vehicles) and other alternatives.

Multiple countries reported surging EV sales. Google Trends have shown rising interest for EVs.

Also worth noting is that sales/inquiries of solar panels have also been rising, and the war is seen to boost further renewable investments. However, oil-fired power plants are already tiny globally (likely <1M b/d total) so the savings from power generation will not be impactful for this analysis.

EVs were already displacing 2-3M b/d of oil pre-crisis. Under business-as-usual trends, IEA expected displacement of ~4M b/d by 2027 and ~5M b/d by 2030. That's about 1.5M b/d of incremental displacement in about 1 year, pre-crisis. Given the trends we've seen above, it's reasonable to expect 2M b/d of displacement post-crisis.

SPR Releases

Member countries of IEA co-ordinated a 400M barrels release from their Strategic reserves.

While this is massive, it is not sustainable in terms of maintaining a balanced market long-term. I see this as a temporary stop-gap until the production ramps up and the permanent demand displacement trends materialize.

Total Supply Gap

Demand destruction is different from demand displacement. Here I'm referring to demand that disappear not because consumers found a replacement, but because they are either priced out or access has been made impossible (such as the 1M b/d of decreased demand from widespread flight cancellations, especially in the Middle East).

For the market to clear, price must rise until supply equals demand. Putting together the above assumptions, my analysis shows that roughly 13-15M b/d of the 20M supply that flowed through Hormuz can be replaced:

Source Est. Incremental Supply (b/d)
Saudi and Nearby Pipelines 8-9M
Venezuela Ramp-up 0.5-1M
US Shale Production 0.5M
OPEC+/Non-OPEC Production 1M
Selective Passage 1-1.5M
Demand Displacement 2M
Total Supply 13-15M

This leaves a gap of about 5-7M b/d in the medium-term, which must be made up by temporary SPR release or demand destruction from higher prices.

In the long-term, persistently high oil price is likely to further accelerate adoption of electric vehicles, and incentivize investments in new pipelines around the Gulf area that could make Hormuz irrelevant altogether.

First and Second Order Effect Investments

My analysis shows that even in one of the worst case scenarios, we are likely to have a manageable situation of elevated oil price but not with a disastrous level of supply shock, as the market is expected to adjust and make up for up to 75% of the disrupted supply in the medium-term.

For those who believe that this will play out, the obvious play is to buy energy ETFs such as Vanguard Energy ETF (VDE) for US-focus, or iShares Global Energy ETF (IXC) for broader international diversification.

I am personally invested in Vermillion Energy (VET) - a Canada-based oil & gas producer with significant exposure to Europe, which is an area most affected by the energy disruption (they're a major importer but unlikely China and India, did not get special permissions to pass tankers through Hormuz). Vermillion's valuation is low at single-digit FCF multiple, which gives investors a good buffer should oil and gas prices correct.

Finally the second order effect is that this disruption will likely accelerate adoption of alternatives such as electric vehicles. Outside from a number of Chinese companies that readers may or may not be comfortable investing in, the only US company with significant market share and exposure to the EV space is Tesla (TSLA). Tesla will likely receive a boon from this renewed interest in EVs.

But should the aforementioned scenario doesn't play out, there are multiple other tailwinds that could make Tesla a very good investment even if this wars ends quickly and permanently.

data.title: Ally Financial: 2026 Off To A Solid Start, Eyes On The Consumer & K Economy
data.description:
Ally Financial (ALLY) shares traded higher by 3% after the consumer-loan company within the Financials sector reported solid Q1 results. The Detroit-based firm tallied record consumer loan applications, helping to fuel a double-beat.

I had a "Buy" rating on ALLY back in September of 2025. Shares are up close to 10% since then, including the premarket gain on Friday, April 17. Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. EPS growth trends are very impressive, while the P/E ratio valuation remains depressed. Still, the technical situation is not ideal, and concerns surround the health of lower- and middle-income consumers.

Auto Loan Delinquencies Ticking Up

K-Economy: Weaker Lower-Income Wage Growth

ALLY Outperforming XLF YoY

In April, Ally reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.11 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.94, while revenue of $2.18 billion, up 5.3% from the same period a year earlier, was a small $40 million beat. Its net financing revenue hit $1.6 billion, up $111 million YoY, while the net interest margin (NIM) ticked up to 3.48%.

Shares rose 3% in the session that followed, marking a third consecutive post-earnings rise. Implied volatility was elevated at 39% coming into the Q1 print, and I expect that to retreat to the low 30s in the coming days. As it stands, the next dividend pay date is May 1, while shares of the $13 billion market cap company sport a low but not immaterial 3.5% short interest.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Ally got off to a strong start to 2026. The company reported GAAP net income of $291 million, while adjusted EPS surged 91% compared to the first quarter of 2025. The robust performance was supported by adjusted net revenue of $2.2 billion and a core pre-tax income of $470 million.

Operational highlights included a record consumer auto applications of 4.4 million, which helped drive $11.5 billion in new consumer originations despite a decline in broader industry sales. Furthermore, Ally maintained a solid capital position with a CET1 ratio of 10.1% and tagged a record adjusted tangible book value per share of $41. So, the balance sheet appears in shape to me.

Ally’s core segments fired on most cylinders, with Auto Finance generating $336 million in pre-tax income and Corporate Finance delivering a 26% ROE. Its retail deposit base also remained a firm foundation, reaching $146 billion and marking 68 consecutive quarters of customer growth, according to the Q1 report. While provision expense for the quarter rose to $468 million due to asset growth, credit trends remained healthy, and the consolidated net charge-off rate actually improved to 1.21%.

Ally: Strong Q1

Looking ahead, the management team was confident in its ability to achieve a net interest margin in the upper 3% range over time, guiding for a full-year 2026 NIM between 3.60% and 3.70%.

Recall that in January, Ally announced a $2 billion share buyback program.

FY 2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be stout in 2026, with further gains in the out year and through FY 2028. Much hinges on operating leverage, so we’ll need to see decent top-line growth in the quarters ahead (we are off to a good start with 2026).

The sellside, meanwhile, has been quite negative on ALLY, as evidenced by a high 13 EPS downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to a lone upgrade. Finally, profitability trends are decent, but they could improve if the current consensus earnings trends come to fruition.

Ally: Revenue & Earnings Estimates, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my September 2025 assumptions. If normalized EPS over the next 12 months comes in close to the consensus figure of $5.60 and achieves the same 9x P/E multiple I assumed in Q3 last year, then shares should trade near $50.

That still leaves a gap between the low market price and the high intrinsic value, but the margin of safety is smaller.

ALLY: Low P/E, But Close To The Long-Term Average

Key risks for Ally include a weaker lower- and middle-income consumer, higher auto delinquency rates if we see a softer domestic macroeconomic environment, and rising interest rates.

Elsewhere, competition remains high in the consumer finance space, while operational risks are many, both at the macro and firm-specific levels.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q2 2026 earnings date of Thursday, July 16, BMO.

Before that, the company hosts its Annual General Meeting on Wednesday, May 6. Shares trade ex a $0.30 dividend on Friday, May 1.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares only about 12% below their fair value in my view, ALLY’s technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that the stock is between support and resistance. I see potential buying pressure down at the $36-$37 zone (the same range I noted in September 2025), while resistance is likely near $48. Both the short-term 50-day moving average and the long-term 200-day moving average are in the middle of that range, with price hovering near both trend-indicator lines.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has risen from technically oversold to overbought in short order, but we haven’t seen a new price high. That’s not as strong a setup compared to the broader market. What’s more, there remains a high amount of volume by price above today’s level, which could give the bulls fits. For now, it’s a neutral chart.

ALLY: Stuck Between Support & Resistance, Flat 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on ALLY. Its valuation remains attractive, but it’s not as compelling as it has been at times in the past 12 months. The technicals, meanwhile, are mixed after a solid Q1 report.

data.title: Ryanair: The Fuel Price Selloff Is Overdone, But Jet Fuel Shortages Are A Risk
data.description:
Amid rising oil prices and potential oil and fuel shortages, airlines in Europe are looking to cut costs. Ryanair Holdings plc (RYAAY) is no exception to that, and particularly for a low-cost carrier this may provide challenges. Since my last report, the company’s share prices have declined by 19%, clearly underperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 1.9% gain. In this report, I discuss Ryanair’s sensitivity to fuel prices, the partial shielding and risks.

Ryanair Has A Higher Proportional Share Of Fuel Costs

Ryanair is always looking to reduce costs, and that may leave Ryanair more exposed to fuel costs, because its fuel costs proportionally may make up a higher share of the operating expenses. In my prior report, I discussed the dispute between Elon Musk and Ryanair centering on the economic feasibility of installing satellite Internet on Ryanair’s fleet, and the company noted that there is a fuel penalty and in combination with the behavior of their cost-conscious customers, the installation of satellite Internet does not make financial sense for Ryanair. Satellite Internet installation requires an external antenna to be mounted on the aircraft, which brings a small fuel burn penalty. With the increase in fuel prices, that fuel burn penalty expressed in dollars is obviously going up. While the dispute may have seemed ridiculous to many, it is with this mindset and ROI scope that Ryanair has been able to run the business efficiently.

I ran the numbers for the prior three financial years, and what we note is that Ryanair’s fuel expenses account for 42%-45% of the operating expenses and 43% over the past three years. Ryanair is not a U.S. operator, but for reference purposes I have looked at the fuel cost exposure of U.S. carriers excluding other costs. That showed that fuel costs were 30% of all costs measured from 2022 until 2024. For mainline carriers, this was 31% and for ultra-low-cost carriers this was 39%. So, the impression that low-cost carriers proportionally have higher fuel costs is true.

The Shielding Mechanisms For Ryanair

The easy way for Ryanair to offset higher costs would be to increase air fares and I believe that will also happen. At the same time, the customers of low-cost carriers are cost-conscious meaning that Ryanair does not have a whole lot of space to increase prices.

That is where the element of shielding from higher cost on a cost level comes into play. There are two simple ways to cut fuel costs:

  1. Consume less fuel.
  2. Hedge against fuel price fluctuations.

Consuming less fuel can be done in two ways. The first one is simply by flying less. The obvious starting point would be to cut loss making routes. However, what also plays a role is that some airports may not be able to supply fuel or fuel in the quantities that Ryanair needs. So, that is also what Ryanair has to navigate. Currently, there is around 6 weeks of fuel left, so there is a big challenge for the summer season.

The other way to consume less fuel is by using fuel efficient airplanes. However, that is not going to help in the near-term in the sense that Ryanair cannot tell Boeing they need more fuel efficient now. In FY25, Ryanair reduced its fuel consumption per available seat-mile by around 1% and current fleet plans suggest similar reductions possible for FY26 and FY27. However, the jet fuel prices in Europe increased from around €2-€2.10 to €4.50 per gallon, which means that prices have more than doubled. A 1% reduction is a drop in the ocean.

Ryanair has around 84% of its Q4 consumption (quarter ending March) hedged at $77 per barrel. That means that 16% of its March consumption is exposed to the higher jet fuel prices. Effectively, that would mean a €2.14 per gallon cost. So, for the final quarter of Ryanair’s economic year there is an impact around four to fourteen eurocents per gallon against the pre-war pricing. That is 2%-7% that Ryanair pays above the pre-war market prices and shows the strength of hedging. At the same time, we note that against pre-war pricing, the expected economic price per gallon would have been €1.74-€1.76. So, for March the increase in the effective price per gallon would be around 22%.

For FY27, the blended price would be €2.08 per gallon which is still in the €2-€2.10 range. So, Ryanair’s hedging strategy leads them to have effective fuel prices that are more or less in line with the pre-war market prices, but the hedging benefit of €0.40-€0.52 per gallon against the pre-war prices is almost fully gone. Additionally, there are additional cost such as fuel handling costs or into-plane costs and carbon costs. So, the full effective impact is even bigger as the gallon prices

Ryanair Stock Remains A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Ryanair, I am maintaining my Buy rating. It is clear that there is a risk, but that risk is not so much that fuel costs are going to explode since the company is largely shielded. However, fuel hedge gains are fading and that is why Ryanair may increase ticket prices and cut some flight activity. Overall, this could mean that the impact on Ryanair’s profits may not be major. However, it depends on how consumer sentiment and availability of jet fuel at European prices evolves. I believe the stock has 32% upside with a $75.77 price target.

If we look at margins, we see that those are holding up quite nicely. CapEx as a share of revenues will be in the 10%-15% range in the years ahead and roughly half of the capital operating cash flow will be used for capital expenditures with an increase in FY28 driven by the introduction of the Boeing 737 MAX 10. Ryanair is in a net cash position, which is a rather unique position for airlines to be in. By FY27, absent of any airplane delivery financing the company will have no bank debt on the balance sheet. This gives the company a lot of room to increase shareholder repurchase and particularly in FY27 there should be around $1.5 billion available to return to shareholders. That has currently not been modelled in when compiling the price targets.

Conclusion: Ryanair Should Be Able To Navigate Cost Impact

For Ryanair, the jet fuel crisis likely will result in fuel hedge gains substantially fading and a substantially higher than expected economic fuel cost per gallon even with 80% of the fuel consumption hedged for FY27. So, what Ryanair is likely doing when cutting costs and raising fares is attempting to offset the spread between new economic fuel cost per which is actually in line with pre-war prices, and the prior assumed economic fuel cost per gallon. For many airlines, higher fuel prices force higher air fares in order to remain profitable. For Ryanair, that is not so much the case I believe. It is more a margin preserving lever that they may pull.

I will be flying Ryanair in two weeks from now, and the main concern right now for me as a passenger, but also for shareholders is whether the airline has to cut flights due to the availability of jet fuel. So, there definitely are risks for Ryanair, but their fuel hedging strategy has proven to be a major de-risk.

data.title: Live Nation: Nothing Is Certain Yet, The Drama Continues
data.description:
Live Nation (LYV) was recently in the news because of a jury verdict that didn’t go its way, with possibilities of a breakup still possible. I wanted to go over the situation in more detail and give my opinion.

The latest on the lawsuit

On April 15th, a federal jury in New York found Live Nation and Ticketmaster illegally maintained monopoly power and overcharged consumers. This comes after a six-week trial with many witnesses, and according to the lawsuit, LYV overcharged customers for $1.72 per ticket over several years. There are also talks of the damages reaching around $700m if this amount is confirmed by the court. The judge has not set the final damages award, with LYV saying that the jury’s verdict is not the last word. It seems that the company has already accrued $280m toward state damages and civil penalties, so if $700m is approved, then it just needs to add another $420m on top.

That is a lot of dough. I am still quite surprised that the lawsuit went against Live Nation. I thought it would be thrown out, and what I am also surprised about is that they only overcharged customers by less than 2 bucks per ticket. Anytime I’m buying a ticket on Ticketmaster, I always feel robbed. It feels like the fees and everything else put the actual ticket price at least $10-$15 higher than it should have been. So, I guess I’m relieved that I’m not really overpaying in the grand scheme of things? I digress. Is the $700m penalty for Live Nation even meaningful enough that it would deter it from making the same mistake again? Let’s take a look at its financial performance and position.

As of FY25, the company’s revenues were $25.2B, up around 9% y/y, and operating income came in at $1.25B, up 51% y/y. So, a one-time charge of $700m would be a decent dent in the company’s performance, but not the end of the world. If we look at the company’s balance sheet, we can see that LYV has around $7B in cash, so that would be a 10% hit to its mountain of cash. It’s not nothing, but I doubt this is going to lead to a change in the company’s practices in the long run.

Ticketmaster Divestiture

To make a real impact on Live Nation, the judge has to look past petty penalties that never amount to any change in behavior. Companies with a boatload of cash will continue to break the rules if paying the penalties is cheaper than changing all of the operations. So, with the verdict of the jury, there is a good chance that Live Nation will have to divest its subsidiary Ticketmaster. So, let’s see how big an impact the divestiture of Ticketmaster is for Live Nation. Live Nation provides a good breakdown of its performance across its many subsidiaries, including Ticketmaster. For Q4, Ticketmaster’s revenues were up 3% to $3.1B. This puts Ticketmaster’s total revenue contributions at around 12%. That is a decent chunk as well, but not the end of the world. So, in theory, if Ticketmaster had to go, we would see a 12% dip in sales. It’s grand. However, if we dig into the profitability aspect, we will see that Ticketmaster is much more valuable to Live Nation than its $3B in sales. Ticketmaster runs at a much higher margin than its Concert revenue segment. LYV’s consolidated Adjusted Operating Income, or AOI, was around $2.36B. $1.134B of it came from Ticketmaster alone. That is around 48% of the total came from a segment that is responsible for only $3B in sales. Concert sales were close to $21B, and it made only $687m in AOI. So, a loss of Ticketmaster is actually going to hit the company’s operations quite a bit when we dig under the surface headline numbers. If that happens, then the company, essentially overnight, will be 48% less profitable.

How likely is the divestiture?

A lot of the reports suggest that the divestiture is not really on the table in modern antitrust cases. Even back when the DOJ was looking at an antitrust case back in January that ended without a resolution, a divestiture of Ticketmaster was highly unlikely. Furthermore, what helps the case of Ticketmaster staying in place right now under the Live Nation brand is that, as part of the DOJ settlement, LYV already has to pay $280m in settlements as well as to divest at least 13 amphitheater booking agreements. This could be enough for the judge, which could be seen as meaningful relief.

However, the jury’s liability also strengthens the states’ hand and gives them a lot more leverage to ask for bigger structural relief in the next phase. The breakup is still on the table, but I wouldn’t say it is the same as probable. I think the only way that the judge will order a breakup is if he honestly believes that this is the only way to restore competition.

Closing Comments

There is no way I am going anywhere near the company’s shares at this time. If there is even a small chance that the company will have to lose its cash cow, it will keep many investors away, including me. This would be a massive operational change, and a re-rating would be very likely to happen for Live Nation. It was already too expensive, so this re-rating is going to be to the downside.

In general, the verdict should be quite positive for the smaller players in this space, and I am looking forward to what that is going to mean for the consumer. I really don’t think we are going to see much lower prices for our concerts, but monopolies are never a good thing in my opinion, so I am hopeful, nonetheless.

data.title: Parnassus Growth Equity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
The strategy pursues strong long-term returns by owning a concentrated portfolio of innovative, high-quality U.S. large cap companies that are well positioned to benefit from long-term secular trends.

Key Takeaways

  • Parnassus Growth Equity Fund (Investor Shares)(PFGEX) returned -9.63% (net of fees) during the quarter, slightly outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 1.12%.
  • Our holdings in the Information Technology, Industrials and Materials sectors boosted relative returns, while holdings in Health Care, Financials and Consumer Discretionary held back relative performance.
  • Amid market volatility due to artificial intelligence (AI) disruption fears and the war in Iran, we moved quickly to re-evaluate potential holdings, take advantage of price dislocations and ensure our overall portfolio positioning remained balanced.
  • Throughout any volatility, we will continue to execute our investment process and seek opportunities to invest in innovative, increasingly relevant and competitively advantaged businesses at attractive valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Market Review

Growth stocks declined significantly in the first quarter, with the Russell 1000 Growth Index ending the quarter in negative territory. Market performance was impacted both by the beginning of a joint military offensive against Iran at the end of February as well as shifting narratives—and increasing concerns—about the potential for AI disruption, particularly in software. Performance varied across sectors, with Energy far outperforming other market segments as the conflict in Iran constrained global supply, pushing oil prices higher. The Financials, Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors posted double-digit losses amid inflationary pressures and negative investor sentiment around the impact of AI. Strength related to AI infrastructure, particularly among manufacturers of semiconductors and related equipment, as well as companies benefiting from electrification, was outweighed by weakness in software, where concerns around AI-driven disruption led to widespread valuation compression.

Quarterly Performance Review

Positioning in Information Technology boosted relative results

The Fund (Investor Shares) returned -9.63% (net of fees) during the quarter, slightly outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index's -9.78%. Our holdings in the Information Technology, Industrials and Materials sectors aided relative returns, while holdings in Health Care, Financials and Consumer Discretionary curbed relative performance.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies semiconductor manufacturing equipment, benefited from sustained demand tied to AI infrastructure build-out, as hyperscalers increased capital spending to support accelerating compute needs.

ASML Holding (ASML), a supplier of equipment for producing advanced microchips, gained on the strength of demand for advanced lithography, reinforcing its essential role in next-generation AI chip production.

GE Vernova (GEV), a leading global energy equipment manufacturer, benefited from surging electricity needs tied to data center build-out amid the continuing growth in AI-related infrastructure.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC)(TSM) central role in AI chip production continued to bolster investor confidence and drive stock gains, as company management announced higher-than-anticipated capital expenditure guidance.

Linde (LIN), a multinational provider of industrial gases, gained as investors favored companies with durable, hard-to-disrupt business models tied to both the global economy and semiconductor manufacturing.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Boston Scientific (BSX), a medical device company, declined on concerns about increased competition and slowing market growth for its atrial fibrillation product, Farapulse, and mixed reaction to overall positive clinical data, though we believe its long-term growth prospects remain intact.

AppFolio (APPF), a provider of cloud-based software for property management, declined amid a broad sell-off within software driven by fears that AI could disrupt traditional software-as-a-service business models.

Atlassian (TEAM) fell along with its software peers as investors believed its emphasis on developer-focused tools presents a risk of AI disruption. We exited the position during the quarter, due to AI disruption concerns and its high valuation.

Ares Management (ARES), an alternative asset manager with a large franchise in private credit, underperformed due to retail redemptions, worries about loans to software private equity buyouts and rising default risk in direct lending.

Salesforce (CRM) stock fell sharply as investor sentiment on software companies broadly weakened amid AI disruption concerns, despite the company's favorable sales figures and margin growth. We view Salesforce as well positioned to capture AI upside given its specialized products and integrated products trading at an attractive valuation.
